concerns.location: plan.md:Phase‑wise Plan & FR/SC Mapping table – Phase 6 (Aggregate Analyses)
concerns.text: No power analysis is provided for the aggregate prevalence estimate (binomial test H₀ : π = 0.05). With the planned minimum corpus (≈100 summaries) the study may be severely under‑powered to detect a modest deviation from 5 % inconsistency, risking a Type II error.

The extraction step assumes that any reported “lift %” can be directly converted to an absolute difference without explicit conversion rules. This may mis‑measure effect sizes when the source reports odds ratios, relative risk, or uses different baselines, threatening construct validity of the `effect_size` variable.

concerns.location: plan.md:FR‑004 description (referenced in Phase 4 – Reconstruction and Phase 5 – Consistency Flagging)
concerns.text: The inconsistency thresholds (absolute p‑value difference > 0.05, relative effect‑size difference > 5 %) are introduced without justification or sensitivity analysis. Arbitrary cut‑offs can inflate false‑positive inconsistency rates and undermine the validity of the prevalence claim.

The synthetic validation dataset is generated programmatically with known ground‑truth statistics, but it does not model the diverse reporting quirks (rounded p‑values, missing confidence intervals, mixed effect‑size units) observed in real‑world A/B test summaries. Consequently, precision/recall measured on this set may not generalize, limiting external validity of the detector’s performance claims.

Aggregate analyses treat all summaries as a homogeneous population, yet source‑wise reporting practices (e.g., tech blogs vs. academic repositories) can differ systematically. Without modeling source as a stratification factor or adjusting for such heterogeneity, confounding may bias the overall inconsistency proportion and the subsequent binomial test.

concerns.location: plan.md:Phase‑wise Plan & FR/SC Mapping table, Phase 2 description
concerns.text: The plan’s Extraction phase describes extracting fields but never names the `ABSummary` entity defined in data-model.md, nor states that the extracted records will conform to the `extracted_summary.schema.yaml` contract. This breaks the Plan ↔ data‑model and Plan ↔ contracts alignment.

Testing is described as '`pytest` + contract validation via `jsonschema`' but does not specify which contracts (audit_record.schema.yaml, extracted_summary.schema.yaml) are validated, leaving the Plan ↔ contracts link ambiguous.

concerns.location: plan.md:Constitution Check table, Principle V row
concerns.text: Principle V (Versioning Discipline) is claimed to be satisfied by noting that artifact hashes are recorded “outside scope of this plan.” The constitution requires every artifact to carry a content hash within the project’s workflow; delegating it outside the plan violates the non‑negotiable requirement.

The aggregate analyses (overall inconsistency proportion, binomial test, Wilson CI, source‑wise Fisher’s exact, temporal proportion‑difference test) treat each audited summary as an independent Bernoulli trial. Summaries are clustered by source domain and by publication month, so the independence assumption is violated. This can inflate type‑I error rates for the binomial test and proportion‑difference tests, making the reported significance levels unreliable. The plan should incorporate a hierarchical or cluster‑robust approach (e.g., mixed‑effects logistic regression, cluster‑bootstrapping, or a design‑based variance estimator) to account for intra‑source correlation before performing hypothesis tests.

concerns.location: plan.md:Phase‑wise Plan & FR/SC Mapping table – Phase 6 (Aggregate Analyses)
concerns.text: No power analysis is provided for the aggregate prevalence estimate (binomial test H₀ : π = 0.05). With the planned minimum corpus (≈100 summaries) the study may be severely under‑powered to detect a modest deviation from 5 % inconsistency, risking a Type II error.

This may mis‑measure effect sizes when the source reports odds ratios, relative risk, or uses different baselines, threatening construct validity of the `effect_size` variable.

concerns.location: plan.md:FR‑004 description (referenced in Phase 4 – Reconstruction and Phase 5 – Consistency Flagging)
concerns.text: The inconsistency thresholds (absolute p‑value difference > 0.05, relative effect‑size difference > 5 %) are introduced without justification or sensitivity analysis. Arbitrary cut‑offs can inflate false‑positive inconsistency rates and undermine the validity of the prevalence claim.

The synthetic validation dataset is generated programmatically with known ground‑truth statistics, but it does not model the diverse reporting quirks (rounded p‑values, missing confidence intervals, mixed effect‑size units) observed in real‑world A/B test summaries. Consequently, precision/recall measured on this set may not generalize, limiting external validity of the detector’s performance claims.

Aggregate analyses treat all summaries as a homogeneous population, yet source‑wise reporting practices (e.g., tech blogs vs. academic repositories) can differ systematically. concerns.location: plan.md:Phase‑wise Plan & FR/SC Mapping table, Phase 2 description
concerns.text: The plan’s Extraction phase describes extracting fields but never names the `ABSummary` entity defined in data-model.md, nor states that the extracted records will conform to the `extracted_summary.schema.yaml` contract. This breaks the Plan ↔ data‑model and Plan ↔ contracts alignment.

Testing is described as '`pytest` + contract validation via `jsonschema`' but does not specify which contracts (audit_record.schema.yaml, extracted_summary.schema.yaml) are validated, leaving the Plan ↔ contracts link ambiguous.

concerns.location: plan.md:Constitution Check table, Principle V row
concerns.text: Principle V (Versioning Discipline) is claimed to be satisfied by noting that artifact hashes are recorded “outside scope of this plan.” The constitution requires every artifact to carry a content hash within the project’s workflow; delegating it outside the plan violates the non‑negotiable requirement.

The aggregate analyses (overall inconsistency proportion, binomial test, Wilson CI, source‑wise Fisher’s exact, temporal proportion‑difference test) treat each audited summary as an independent Bernoulli trial. Summaries are clustered by source domain and by publication month, so the independence assumption is violated. This can inflate type‑I error rates for the binomial test and proportion‑difference tests, making the reported significance levels unreliable. The plan should incorporate a hierarchical or cluster‑robust approach (e.g., mixed‑effects logistic regression, cluster‑bootstrapping, or a design‑based variance estimator) to account for intra‑source correlation before performing hypothesis tests.

The plan states: "Perform a one‑sided binomial test (H₀ : π = 0.05) using the robust SE." Applying a cluster‑robust variance estimator to an exact binomial test is not a standard or statistically valid approach, because the binomial test assumes independent Bernoulli trials and does not incorporate a standard error that can be adjusted for clustering. A more appropriate method would be a cluster‑adjusted proportion test (e.g., using generalized estimating equations or a design‑based variance estimator) rather than modifying the binomial test with a robust SE.
